retrieved_docs.title: Goodpasture Syndrome -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Light microscopy will show crescentic glomerulonephritis. All lesions in anti-GBM disease are generally in the same nephritic stage. Interstitial inflammation is also found mainly in the periglomerular region. In the later stages, fibrosis develops rapidly over a few days to weeks, manifesting as glomerular sclerosis and obliteration. Immunofluorescence stains are more specific and diagnostic, usually exhibiting bright linear deposits of immunoglobulin G (IgG) and complement (C3) on the glomerular basement membrane. Predominantly, the subclass IgG-1 is present. [19] Staining of the renal tubular basement membrane is also possible in addition to glomerular staining. [20] See Images. Crescentic Glomerulonephritis, Immunofluorescence Staining for IgG. Although lung biopsy is not commonly performed, if conducted, similar staining of the alveolar basement membrane can also be observed. The biopsy may also show extensive hemorrhage with deposition of hemosiderin-laden macrophages within alveolar spaces. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Rapidly Progressive Glomerulonephritis -- Etiology -- Anti-Glomerular Basement Membrane Disease
retrieved_docs.content: Circulating immunoglobulin G (IgG) antibodies are directed against an antigen normally present in the GBM and alveolar basement membrane, specifically the non-collagenous domain of the α-3 chain of type IV collagen. This anti-GBM antibody deposition accounts for approximately 10% to 15% of diffuse crescentic glomerulonephritis cases. About 40% to 60% of these cases are associated with alveolar hemorrhage, a condition known as Goodpasture syndrome. [3] Fewer than 10% of anti-GBM disease cases present with isolated pulmonary involvement. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Rapidly Progressive Glomerulonephritis -- Etiology -- Double-Positive Antibody Disease
retrieved_docs.content: Also called dual antibody disease, this type of crescentic glomerulonephritis is associated with a positive ANCA and anti-GBM antibody. Some studies indicate that 10% to 50% of patients with anti-GBM disease have detectable ANCA, typically anti-MPO. In contrast, up to 10% of patients with ANCA-associated vasculitis also have circulating anti-GBM antibodies. [5] Generally, the positive ANCA precedes the anti-GBM antibodies; therefore, it is postulated that ANCA leads to the anti-GBM antibodies by exposing epitopes on the GBM. Renal manifestations follow an anti-GBM pattern, whereas systemic symptoms are similar to those of ANCA vasculitis. [9] [10] This double-positive characteristic comes into play during treatment selection. [11] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Granulomatosis With Polyangiitis -- Etiology -- Double-Positive Antibody Disease
retrieved_docs.content: Also called dual antibody disease, this type of crescentic glomerulonephritis is associated with a positive ANCA and anti-glomerular basement membrane (GBM) antibody. Some studies indicate that 10% to 50% of patients with anti-GBM disease have detectable ANCA, typically anti-MPO. In contrast, up to 10% of patients with ANCA-associated vasculitis also have circulating anti-GBM antibodies. [12] Generally, the positive ANCA precedes the anti-GBM antibodies; therefore, it is postulated that ANCA leads to the anti-GBM antibodies by exposing epitopes on the GBM. Renal manifestations follow an anti-GBM pattern, whereas systemic symptoms are similar to those of ANCA vasculitis. [13] [14] This double-positive characteristic comes into play during treatment selection. [8] [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Goodpasture Syndrome -- Pathophysiology -- Double-Positive Antibody Disease
retrieved_docs.content: Also called dual antibody disease, this type of crescentic glomerulonephritis is associated with a positive antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibody (ANCA) test and anti-GBM antibody. Some study results have indicated that 10% to 50% of patients with anti-GBM disease have detectable ANCA, typically anti-MPO. In contrast, up to 10% of patients with ANCA-associated vasculitis also have circulating anti-GBM antibodies. [15] Generally, the positive ANCA precedes the anti-GBM antibodies; therefore, it is postulated that ANCA leads to the anti-GBM antibodies by exposing epitopes on the GBM. Patients with double-positive disease are often older, have longer prodromes, and have frequent relapses—similar to ANCA-associated vasculitis. Renal manifestations follow an anti-GBM pattern, whereas systemic symptoms are similar to those of ANCA vasculitis. [16] This double-positive characteristic comes into play during treatment selection. [17] [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Crescentic Glomerulonephritis (Archived) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Type 1 (anti-glomerular basement membrane [GBM] disease) presents with linear deposits of immunoglobulin G (IgG) Type 2 (immune-complex mediated) presents with granular deposits of immunoglobulin Type 3 (pauci-immune) presents with few or no immune deposits, antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibody-associated small-vessel vasculitis (SVV) that may be renal-limited or part of a systemic disease, for example, granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA). Type 4 includes combinations of types 1 and 3 Type 5 is ANCA-negative, pauci-immune renal vasculitis (5% to 10% of cases)

retrieved_docs.title: Rapidly Progressive Glomerulonephritis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Some studies found that in patients with double-positive antibodies (positive for anti-GBM and ANCA), the renal manifestations follow an anti-GBM pattern clinically and histologically, whereas extrarenal manifestations are similar to those of ANCA-associated vasculitis. [6] [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Idiopathic Pulmonary Hemosiderosis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Group 1 pulmonary hemosiderosis is defined by pulmonary hemorrhage associated with circulating anti-glomerular basement membrane (anti-GBM) antibodies. Anti-GBM diseases are small vessel vasculitis affecting the capillary system, where there is immunoglobulin and complement deposition along basement membranes of primarily the lungs and the kidneys such as in Goodpasture syndrome. A majority of these patients will develop glomerular crescent formation with rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis; however, on average, 40%-60% of patients with anti-GBM diseases will develop an alveolar hemorrhage. Diffuse alveolar hemorrhage presents clinically in these patients, and a diagnostic broncho-alveolar lavage may demonstrate hemosiderin-laden macrophages. Unlike idiopathic pulmonary hemosiderosis, group 1 pulmonary hemosiderosis is stratified based on kidney biopsy, showing linear deposits of IgG under direct immunofluorescence. Lung biopsy samples are not used in the diagnosis of anti-GBM disease and would likely have no specific information. [2] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Diffuse Proliferative Glomerulonephritis(Archived) -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The etiology of diffuse proliferative glomerulonephritis stems from the type and location of deposits seen on renal biopsy; the most commonly associated disease is systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE). Class IV of lupus nephritis shows a diffuse pattern on microscopic examination. In addition, the renal involvement seen in IgA nephropathy, anti-glomerular basement membrane (anti-GBM) antibody disease, pauci immune vasculitis like granulomatosis with polyangiitis, microscopic polyangiitis, and Henoch-Schonlein purpura all show a histological pattern indicative of DPGN. Some forms of post-infectious glomerulonephritis, especially those associated with chronic infections like endocarditis, hepatitis B, and C, show a diffuse involvement of glomeruli with increased cellular proliferation. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Rapidly Progressive Glomerulonephritis -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: All lesions in anti-GBM disease are generally in the same nephritic stage. Interstitial inflammation is also found mainly in the periglomerular region. In the later stages, fibrosis develops rapidly over a few days to weeks, manifesting as glomerular sclerosis and obliteration. Immunofluorescence staining in anti-GBM disease shows linear IgG deposition along the glomeruli capillary walls. Staining of the renal tubular basement membrane is also possible. [6] Although lung biopsy is not commonly performed, if conducted, similar staining of the alveolar basement membrane can also be observed. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Rapidly Progressive Glomerulonephritis -- Etiology -- Anti-Glomerular Basement Membrane Disease
retrieved_docs.content: A small percentage of patients present with mild renal dysfunction, proteinuria, and hematuria. When these patients undergo a biopsy, it confirms the presence of anti-GBM disease, with linear IgG deposition on the GBM, but without crescenting glomerulonephritis. In addition, these patients can test negative for anti-GBM antibodies. [3] Other rare cases of diffuse alveolar hemorrhage or renal insufficiency have been reported with negative anti-GBM antibodies. [4] Please see Statpearls' companion reference, " Goodpasture Syndrome, " for further information.

retrieved_docs.title: ANCA-Associated Vasculitis -- Pathophysiology -- Double-Positive Antibody Disease
retrieved_docs.content: Also called dual antibody disease, this type of crescentic glomerulonephritis is associated with a positive ANCA and anti-GBM antibody. Some studies indicate that 10% to 50% of patients with anti-GBM disease have detectable ANCA, typically anti-MPO. In contrast, up to 10% of patients with ANCA-associated vasculitis also have circulating anti-GBM antibodies. [19] Generally, the positive ANCA precedes the anti-GBM antibodies; therefore, it is postulated that ANCA leads to the anti-GBM antibodies by exposing epitopes on the GBM. Renal manifestations follow an anti-GBM pattern, whereas systemic symptoms are similar to those of ANCA vasculitis. [20] [21] This double-positive characteristic comes into play during treatment selection. [15] [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Diffuse Proliferative Glomerulonephritis(Archived) -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: The characteristic wire-looping pattern of capillary walls is also noted. Electron microscopy shows the precise location of these electron-dense deposits. These deposits can be present in the subepithelial, subendothelial, or intramembranous spaces. In some cases, there is the involvement of the tuboreticular spaces. Immunofluorescence findings are indicative of the etiology of DPGN as in anti-GBM disease, it shows linear deposits along the basement membrane, while in others it shows granular deposits of immune-complex. Staining also shows the presence of immunoglobulins, fibrin, or complements or the absence of these, as seen in pauci-immune ANCA-related DPGN. It can also show which immunoglobulin is present as in IgA immunoglobulins seen in IgA nephropathy or IgG4 or IgG1 seen more commonly in DPGN associated with lupus nephritis or IgG, IgM, C3, and C1q seen in others. A renal biopsy finding also tells about the chronicity, severity, and extent of renal damage depending on the presence of crescents, necrosis, or exudative lesions. [9] [10] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Nephritic Syndrome -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Crescentic Glomerulonephritis: The crescent is formed by the proliferation of epithelial cells in the parietal layer of the Bowman capsule. Macrophages, fibrin exudates, fibroblasts, complement products may be seen trapped in the mesangium and the Bowman's capsule space. [14] In addition to this, other features of disorder-specific lesions may be seen in different diseases. In Goodpasture syndrome, there is a linear deposition of IgG antibodies and C3 along the GBM. Lupus nephritis has a "full-house pattern" with IgG, IgM, IgA, C3 immune deposits present diffusely in the mesangium, sub epithelium, and sub-endothelium. [15] Anti-GBM antibodies or immune complexes are not detected by immunofluorescence and electron microscopy in pauci-immune crescentic glomerulonephritis. Vasculitis may be seen on renal biopsy.

retrieved_docs.title: Rapidly Progressive Glomerulonephritis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: RPGN and crescentic glomerulonephritis are often used interchangeably, and the progression of crescent formation can be associated with irreversible renal function loss. Generally, crescentic glomerulonephritis is defined as crescents affecting over half of the glomeruli; however, even a single crescent can potentially signify an underlying pathological process. Early diagnosis and treatment are crucial to prevent further renal function loss. RPGN is broadly classified based on histopathology and immune complex deposition. [1] [2] Type I: Anti-glomerular basement membrane (GBM) linear antibody deposition disease. Type II: Granular immune complex deposition disorders. Type III: Pauci-immune disease (absence of deposition, generally anti-neutrophil cytoplasmic antibody (ANCA)-positive vasculitis). Type IV: Double antibody positive for both ANCA and anti-GBM. Some mixed variants are also reported with features of different types. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Goodpasture Syndrome -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Some clinicians may interchangeably use several terms, including anti-glomerular basement membrane disease, Goodpasture syndrome, and Goodpasture disease. [1] Anti-GBM disease can also refer to the presence of renal disease without pulmonary involvement, while Goodpasture disease or syndrome always has pulmonary involvement. Some clinicians even describe coexistent renal and pulmonary disease of any kind as "Goodpasture Syndrome," but in general, this term refers to anti-GBM antibodies. About 40% to 60% of cases are associated with alveolar hemorrhage, while fewer than 10% of anti-GBM disease cases present with isolated pulmonary involvement. [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: glomerulonephritis is associated with داء مضاد للغشاء القاعدي الكبيبي and also has a relationship with متلازمة رئوية كلوية, highlighting that severe renal inflammation often co‑exists with anti‑GBM disease and the pulmonary‑renal syndrome. Together, these interconnected concepts illustrate how systemic autoimmune and vascular disorders can manifest with characteristic retinal hemorrhages, thromboses, and broader organ involvement, emphasizing the importance of integrated diagnostic and therapeutic strategies in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 564
retrieved_docs.content: Necrotizing glomerulonephritis, a severe form of التهاب كبيبات الكلى (glomerulonephritis), is closely associated with داء مضاد للغشاء القاعدي الكبيجي (anti‑basement membrane disease of the kidney). This anti‑GBM disease commonly manifests as اختلال وظيفة الكلى (renal dysfunction) and is a central component of متلازمة رئوية كلوية (pulmonary‑renal syndrome). The interrelationship among necrotizing glomerulonephritis, داء مضاد للغشاء القاعدي الكبيجي, اختلال وظيفة الكلى, and متلازمة رئوية كلوية highlights the need for integrated diagnostic and therapeutic strategies to address both renal and pulmonary involvement.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 738
retrieved_docs.content: Necrotizing glomerulonephritis is a specific, severe form of التهاب كبيبات الكلى (glomerulonephritis) that is closely linked to داء مضاد للغشاء القاعدي الكبيبي (anti‑basement membrane disease). This association reflects the pathogenic role of anti‑GBM antibodies, which can produce both renal injury and the متلازمة رئوية كلوية (pulmonary‑renal syndrome). As a manifestation of اختلال وظيفة الكلى (renal dysfunction), necrotizing glomerulonephritis often progresses to chronic kidney impairment, clinically associated with قصور كلوي مزمن (chronic renal failure). The disease spectrum encompassed by the collective term nephritis; nephrosis; renal sclerosis includes a range of inflammatory and sclerotic renal disorders. These conditions share the glomerulus as their primary site of pathology (finding_site_of glomerulus) and are mapped to التهاب كبيبات الكلى بعد الالتهاب العقدي (post‑infectious glomerulonephritis), highlighting a common pathway of glomerular injury following immune‑mediated inflammation. Within this broader group, متلازمة التفاعل الدوائي المترافق مع كثرة اليوزينيات والأعراض الجهازية (SMQ) (drug‑induced hypersensitivity syndrome with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms) is recognized as a member, underscoring the potential for systemic drug reactions to precipitate renal inflammation and sclerosis. Overall, necrotizing glomerulonephritis and its related entities illustrate the interconnected nature of immune‑mediated renal diseases, their systemic manifestations, and the importance of recognizing both primary glomerular pathology and secondary contributors such as drug‑induced syndromes for comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2262
retrieved_docs.content: Necrotizing glomerulonephritis is associated with داء مضاد للغشاء القاعدي الكبيبي (anti‑basement membrane disease), and it also has a relationship with متلازمة رئوية كلوية (pulmonary‑renal syndrome). داء مضاد للغشاء القاعدي الكبيبي manifests as اختلال وظيفة الكلى (renal dysfunction), and this renal impairment is a central feature of the pulmonary‑renal syndrome that accompanies necrotizing glomerulonephritis. Recognizing that necrotizing glomerulonephritis, anti‑GBM disease, renal dysfunction, and pulmonary‑renal syndrome are interlinked helps clinicians diagnose and manage the combined kidney‑lung involvement more effectively.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 917
retrieved_docs.content: Necrotizing glomerulonephritis is closely linked to anti‑basement membrane disease, a condition that manifests as renal dysfunction, and it is also a key component of pulmonary‑renal syndrome. This form of glomerular injury encompasses the entities “nephritis; nephrosis; renal sclerosis,” which are localized to the glomerulus and represent specific types of proliferative glomerulonephritis. These three entities are further mapped to post‑infectious glomerulonephritis, indicating that they share pathological features with glomerular inflammation that follows an infectious episode. Proliferative glomerulonephritis, the broader category that includes “nephritis; nephrosis; renal sclerosis,” also contains systemic lupus erythematosus (SMQ) as a member, highlighting the autoimmune contribution to this pattern of kidney disease. Together, these relationships illustrate how necrotizing glomerulonephritis, anti‑basement membrane disease, pulmonary‑renal syndrome, post‑infectious glomerulonephritis, and systemic lupus erythematosus are interconnected through shared glomerular pathology and renal functional impairment, underscoring the importance of integrated diagnostic and therapeutic approaches in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2623

This interrelationship highlights the importance of recognizing necrotizing glomerulonephritis not only as a severe renal pathology but also as a manifestation of systemic autoimmune processes that can affect multiple organ systems, guiding comprehensive diagnostic evaluation and coordinated therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2764
retrieved_docs.content: Necrotizing glomerulonephritis is a severe form of التهاب كبيبات الكلى (glomerulonephritis) characterized by necrosis of glomerular capillaries. It is closely associated with داء مضاد للغشاء القاعدي الكبيبي (anti‑basement membrane disease), an autoimmune disorder that targets the glomerular basement membrane and underlies conditions such as Goodpasture’s syndrome. Additionally, necrotizing glomerulonephritis often presents as part of متلازمة رئية كلوية (pulmonary‑renal syndrome), a clinical picture in which simultaneous lung hemorrhage and renal injury occur. Understanding these interconnections helps clinicians identify the underlying autoimmune mechanisms and select appropriate immunosuppressive and supportive therapies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2447
retrieved_docs.content: Necrotizing glomerulonephritis is classified as a form of اعتلال كبيبات الكلى (glomerular disease) and is frequently encountered as a component of متلازمة رئوية كلوية (pulmonary‑renal syndrome). As a severe, necrotizing inflammation of the renal glomeruli, it represents a specific subtype within the broader category of glomerular pathology. Its association with pulmonary‑renal syndrome highlights the clinical pattern in which simultaneous lung hemorrhage and rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis occur, often due to small‑vessel vasculitis or autoimmune mechanisms. Recognizing necrotizing glomerulonephritis as both a distinct glomerular disease and a manifestation of pulmonary‑renal syndrome is essential for accurate diagnosis, prompt immunosuppressive treatment, and coordinated care of the intertwined renal and pulmonary complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 821

It is closely associated with داء مضاد للغشاء القاعدي الكبيبي (anti‑basement membrane disease), reflecting a pathogenic link to auto‑immune injury of the glomerular basement membrane. In clinical practice, necrotizing glomerulonephritis often presents as part of متلازمة رئوية كلوية (pulmonary‑renal syndrome), indicating simultaneous involvement of the lungs and kidneys. The progression of اعتلال كبيبات الكلى can lead to اليوريمية (uremia), underscoring the potential for severe renal failure if the underlying glomerular injury is not promptly recognized and treated. Understanding these interconnections is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and coordinated management of patients with necrotizing glomerulonephritis and related systemic manifestations.

Additionally, اختلال وظيفة الكلى (renal dysfunction) is recognized as a manifestation of داء مضاد للغشاء القاعدي الكبيبي, underscoring the functional impairment that accompanies this autoimmune renal pathology. Understanding these interrelationships is essential for accurate diagnosis, comprehensive management, and targeted therapeutic strategies in patients presenting with necrotizing glomerulonephritis and related pulmonary‑renal complications.

Necrotizing glomerulonephritis is a specialized form of glomerulonephritis (inverse isa relationship) and, together with other forms of glomerular inflammation (التهاب كبيبات الكلى), is clinically associated with chronic renal failure. Moreover, anti‑basement‑membrane disease is described as a manifestation of kidney‑function disturbance (اختلال وظيفة الكلى), reinforcing the connection between immune‑mediated glomerular injury and progressive renal dysfunction. Collectively, these relationships emphasize how cardiac, renal, metabolic, gastrointestinal, and neuropsychiatric conditions intersect through shared concepts of chronicity, impairment, and anatomical focus, informing integrated patient assessment and multidisciplinary management.

Necrotizing glomerulonephritis is a specific form of glomerular inflammation (التهاب كبيبات الكلى) that is associated with anti‑basement‑membrane disease (داء مضاد للغشاء القاعدي الكبيبي), a condition that also manifests as renal dysfunction (اختلال وظيفة الكلى). This necrotizing process frequently occurs within the context of a pulmonary‑renal syndrome (متلازمة رئوية كلوية), underscoring the close relationship between lung and kidney involvement in certain autoimmune disorders. The disease spectrum that includes nephritis, nephrosis, and renal sclerosis is situated at the glomerulus, the primary site of injury in these glomerular disorders. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 902
retrieved_docs.content: Necrotizing glomerulonephritis is a severe, necrotizing form of التهاب كبيبات الكلى (glomerulonephritis) and is classified under اعتلال كبيبات الكلى (renal glomerular disease). It is closely associated with داء مضاد للغشاء القاعدي الكبيبي (anti‑basement membrane disease), a condition that can manifest as اختلال وظيفة الكلى (renal dysfunction). In addition, necrotizing glomerulonephritis has a clinical relationship with متلازمة رئوية كلوية (pulmonary‑renal syndrome), reflecting its potential to involve both the kidneys and the lungs. Within the broader spectrum of اعتلال كبيبات الكلى, the disease can progress to اليوريمية (uremia), underscoring the risk of severe renal impairment. Together, these interconnections highlight how necrotizing glomerulonephritis, anti‑basement membrane disease, renal dysfunction, pulmonary‑renal syndrome, and uremia are linked within the pathophysiology of renal glomerular disorders, informing diagnosis, monitoring, and comprehensive patient care.

The drug‑interaction SMQ is also a member of the nephritis/nephrosis/renal‑sclerosis group, while acute kidney inflammation (التهاب الكلية الحاد) belongs to the الفشل الكلوي الحاد SMQ, highlighting how overlapping glomerular injury, systemic drug‑reaction syndromes, and acute inflammatory processes collectively drive renal dysfunction and failure, necessitating integrated diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.
